summary: Cache.entries is a bare Go map accessed by Get and Put with no mutex or other synchronization. Any concurrent use (the normal case for a resolver response cache serving multiple in-flight lookups) will trigger a fatal 'concurrent map read and map write' panic or be flagged by -race, directly violating REQ-006/AC-006. No test file was included to demonstrate concurrency safety or any other behavior.

findings.claim: Cache.entries (a plain map[string]cacheEntry) is read in Get and written in Put with no mutex, RWMutex, sync.Map, or other synchronization primitive guarding access.
findings.expected: Per REQ-006, the cache MUST be concurrency-safe and pass `go test -race` under concurrent access. Concurrent Get/Put or Put/Put calls from multiple goroutines must not race.
findings.actual: Cache struct has a single field `entries map[string]cacheEntry` and no lock. Get performs `c.entries[key]` and Put performs `c.entries[key] = ...` directly. Go maps are not safe for concurrent read/write or concurrent write/write; this will either panic with 'fatal error: concurrent map read and map write' at runtime or be flagged by the race detector.

reproducer.ref: Spawn N goroutines calling c.Put(key, msg, ttl, now) concurrently with M goroutines calling c.Get(key, now) on a shared *Cache instance, run with `go test -race`; expect a DATA RACE report or a runtime fatal error on concurrent map access.

findings.suggested_remediation: Add a sync.RWMutex (or sync.Mutex) field to Cache; take a read lock (RLock/RUnlock) around the map read in Get and a write lock (Lock/Unlock) around the map write in Put. Alternatively use sync.Map if the access pattern favors that.

findings.claim: No test file (e.g. cache_test.go) is included with the candidate, so there is no automated verification that Get/Put are concurrency-safe or -race clean.
findings.expected: AC-006 requires the cache to be verified concurrency-safe and -race clean; a corresponding test exercising concurrent Get/Put under `go test -race` should be part of the change.
findings.actual: The bundle contains only internal/resolver/cache.go with no accompanying test file, so REQ-006 compliance is unverified and, given F-001, would in fact fail if tested.
findings.suggested_remediation: Add internal/resolver/cache_test.go with a concurrent-access test (multiple goroutines calling Get/Put on a shared Cache) run under `go test -race` in CI, plus basic expiry/hit/miss unit tests.

findings.claim: Expired entries are only removed lazily when Get is called for that exact key; entries that are Put but never subsequently Get with a matching key (or Get after expiry with a different key pattern) remain in the map forever, growing memory usage unboundedly.
findings.expected: A response cache typically bounds memory via periodic sweep of expired entries, an LRU/size cap, or similar, to avoid unbounded growth over the process lifetime.
findings.actual: No eviction path exists other than the check-and-skip in Get; Put never removes anything and there is no background sweeper or max-size enforcement.
findings.suggested_remediation: Add a periodic cleanup goroutine (with proper locking per F-001) or a max-entries/LRU eviction policy to bound memory growth.

unreviewed_areas: Caller sites that invoke Cache.Get/Put (not included in bundle) and whether they actually run concurrently in production, which would confirm real-world exploitability of F-001.
Any DNS message serialization/validation logic upstream of the cache (msg []byte contents) — out of scope for this file.
Overall resolver package integration and how TTL/now values are sourced.
evidence_gaps: No test file was provided in the bundle, so it cannot be confirmed whether a race test was run/expected elsewhere in the repo but simply omitted from this review bundle.
No information on expected concurrency level (e.g., goroutines per request) to size the severity of the unbounded-growth concern (F-003) more precisely.
